title: Cotula minuta
intro: Cotula minuta, also known as Small Dusty Miller or Small Buttonweed, is a small annual or perennial plant in the daisy family (Asteraceae) that is native to Australia and New Zealand. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Cotula minuta include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Cotula minuta are teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues and skin problems.
taxonomy: Cotula minuta is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Cotula, which is a group of small, annual plants native to Australia. The plant is commonly known as small bugloss or small ragwort. As a member of the Asteraceae family, it is related to other plants such as daisies and sunflowers. The classification of Cotula minuta reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants within the family.
uses: Cotula minuta is a species of plant that has been traditionally used in folk medicine. Its leaves and stems are used to make a tea, which is believed to have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant is said to be effective in treating various health issues, including fever, colds, and skin conditions. In some cultures, Cotula minuta is also used to treat respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and coughs. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of this plant.
benefits: Cotula minuta, also known as dwarf cotula, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues. Its leaves and flowers are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help to reduce the risk of chronic diseases. The plant is believed to have antimicrobial properties, which could aid in the treatment of infections and wounds. properties: Cotula minuta, also known as the button weed or small buttonweed, is native to southern Africa and has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its therapeutic properties include being used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs, as well as skin problems like eczema and acne. The plant has anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that contribute to its healing effects. It is also used to treat gastrointestinal issues and as a natural remedy for fever. Further research is needed to fully understand the extent of Cotula minuta's therapeutic properties.
constituents:
Cotula minuta is also known as the pyrethrum chrysanthemum or lesser marsh daisy. 
Cotula minuta  contains active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones such as pyrethrin I and II, as well as other pyrethrins that exhibit insecticidal properties. 
These compounds are known for their ability to disrupt insect nervous systems and are often used in natural pest control products. 
In addition to pyrethrins, Cotula minuta may also contain other bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and terpenoids. 
The exact composition and potency of Cotula minuta 's active constituents can vary depending on factors such as the plant's origin and growing conditions.
parts: Cotula minuta, also known as buttonweed or button lettuce, is a plant with a variety of medicinal properties. The entire plant, including its leaves, stems, and roots, can be used to create medicinal remedies. However, the most commonly used parts for medicinal purposes are the leaves and roots of the plant. These parts have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems and skin conditions. The medicinal properties of Cotula minuta are attributed to its high concentration of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids.
preparations: Cotula minuta, also known as the tiny buttercup or small cotula, has been traditionally used in herbal preparations to treat various ailments. The plant's leaves and flowers are used to make teas, infusions, and poultices that are said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Some herbalists recommend using Cotula minuta to treat minor skin irritations and wounds due to its purported ability to promote healing and reduce inflammation. Additionally, the plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat digestive issues and fever. However, it is essential to consult with a qualified healthcare professional or herbalist before using Cotula minuta in any herbal preparation.
side_effects: Cotula minuta is commonly known as buttonweed, and its side effects are not well-documented. However, it is considered an invasive weed and can cause skin irritation in some individuals. If ingested, it may cause gastrointestinal problems, such as nausea and vomiting. The plant's sap may also cause skin redness and itching in some people. It is recommended to handle the plant with caution and avoid ingestion.
